question: What was the Central Government Debt as a percentage of GDP at the end of 2021-22?

expected.answer_summary: The Central Government Debt at the end of 2021-22 was 58.59 per cent of GDP, which was a reduction from the peak of 61.55 per cent in 2020-21. The target under the FRBM Act is to limit Central Government Debt to 40 per cent of GDP by the end of 2024-25.
key_facts: Central Government debt reduced to 58.59 per cent of GDP in 2021-22
This was down from peak of 61.55 per cent in 2020-21
Target under FRBM Act is 40 per cent of GDP by end of 2024-25

key_amounts.context: Central Government Debt at end of 2021-22

question: What was the total Central Government Debt in absolute terms at the end of 2021-22?

expected.answer_summary: The total Central Government Debt at the end of 2021-22 was ₹138.65 lakh crore as computed by audit based on the FRBM Act definition. This comprised Internal Debt of ₹114.62 lakh crore, External Debt of ₹6.58 lakh crore at current exchange rate, Public Account Liability of ₹16.44 lakh crore, EBR of ₹1.39 lakh crore, minus Cash Balance of ₹0.38 lakh crore.
key_facts: Central Government Debt was ₹138.65 lakh crore at end of 2021-22
Internal Debt was ₹114.62 lakh crore
External Debt at current exchange rate was ₹6.58 lakh crore
Public Account Liability was ₹16.44 lakh crore

key_amounts.context: External Debt at current exchange rate

question: What was the General Government Debt as a percentage of GDP in 2021-22?

expected.answer_summary: The General Government Debt stood at 82.63 per cent of GDP at the end of 2021-22. This was a decline from the all-time high of 87.91 per cent during 2020-21. The target set by the FRBM Act is to limit General Government Debt to 60 per cent of GDP by 2024-25.
key_facts: General Government Debt was 82.63 per cent of GDP at end of 2021-22
This was down from 87.91 per cent in 2020-21
Target is 60 per cent of GDP by 2024-25

key_amounts.context: General Government Debt at end of 2021-22

must_not_contain: Central Government Debt percentage confused with General Government Debt

question: What was the actual Fiscal Deficit for 2021-22 and how did it compare to the budget estimates?

expected.answer_summary: The actual Fiscal Deficit for 2021-22 was 7.4 per cent of GDP as per actuals, against the Budget Estimate of 6.8 per cent and Revised Estimate of 6.9 per cent. As per UGFA calculation, the Fiscal Deficit was ₹17.63 lakh crore (7.45 per cent of GDP), while Budget at a Glance showed it as ₹15.85 lakh crore (6.70 per cent of GDP). The variation was due to inclusion of certain transactions in UGFA like Back to Back Loans to States in lieu of GST Compensation shortfall.
key_facts: Actual Fiscal Deficit was 7.4 per cent of GDP
Budget Estimate was 6.8 per cent of GDP
Revised Estimate was 6.9 per cent of GDP
Fiscal Deficit as per UGFA was ₹17.63 lakh crore
Fiscal Deficit as per BAG was ₹15.85 lakh crore

question: What was the amount of additional guarantees given by the Government in 2021-22 and was it within the prescribed limit?

expected.answer_summary: During FY 2021-22, the additional guarantee given by the Government was ₹0.96 lakh crore or 0.40 per cent of GDP, which was well within the limit of 0.5 per cent of GDP set under the FRBM Act, 2003. The total outstanding guarantee at the end of FY 2021-22 was ₹5.19 lakh crore (2.19 per cent of GDP).
key_facts: Additional guarantee in 2021-22 was ₹0.96 lakh crore
This was 0.40 per cent of GDP
Within the FRBM limit of 0.5 per cent of GDP
Total outstanding guarantee was ₹5.19 lakh crore

key_amounts.context: Additional guarantee as percentage of GDP
Total outstanding guarantee at end of 2021-22

question: What was the Gross Tax Revenue collection in 2021-22 compared to Budget Estimates?

expected.answer_summary: The Gross Tax Revenue collection in FY 2021-22 was ₹27.09 lakh crore, which was 22.19 per cent higher than the Budget Estimate of ₹22.17 lakh crore. This robust collection led to overall revenue receipt collection of ₹24.36 lakh crore against budgeted ₹21.05 lakh crore.
key_facts: Gross Tax Revenue collected was ₹27.09 lakh crore
Budget Estimate was ₹22.17 lakh crore
Collection was 22.19 per cent higher than BE
Overall revenue receipts were ₹24.36 lakh crore

question: What was the status of disinvestment receipts in 2021-22?

expected.answer_summary: The Budget Estimate 2021-22 projected ₹1.75 lakh crore from disinvestment but only ₹0.15 lakh crore was actually collected. Of these actual receipts, the Government received ₹0.08 lakh crore from disinvestment of its equity in PSUs. This included sale of stake in NMDC (₹3,654 crore), HUDCO (₹720 crore), Hindustan Copper (₹742 crore), strategic disinvestment of Air India (₹2,700 crore), sale of IPCL shares (₹219 crore), and buyback of MOIL shares (₹394 crore).
key_facts: Budget Estimate projected ₹1.75 lakh crore from disinvestment
Actual collection was only ₹0.15 lakh crore
₹0.08 lakh crore came from disinvestment of equity in PSUs
Strategic disinvestment of Air India yielded ₹2,700 crore

question: What was the tax revenue raised but not realized in 2021-22?

expected.answer_summary: In FY 2021-22, tax revenue raised but not realized was ₹15.83 lakh crore, which was 58.42 per cent of total tax collected. This was an improvement from ₹16.19 lakh crore (68.81 per cent) in FY 2020-21. Of this, ₹12.03 lakh crore was under dispute (including ₹10.36 lakh crore for Income Tax) and ₹3.80 lakh crore was not under dispute (including ₹3.54 lakh crore for Income Tax).
key_facts: Tax revenue raised but not realized was ₹15.83 lakh crore
This was 58.42 per cent of total tax collected
Amount under dispute was ₹12.03 lakh crore
Amount not under dispute was ₹3.80 lakh crore
Income Tax under dispute was ₹10.36 lakh crore

key_amounts.context: Tax revenue raised but not realized in 2021-22
As percentage of total tax collected

question: What was the Capital Expenditure in 2021-22 and how did it change from 2020-21?

expected.answer_summary: Capital expenditure increased significantly from ₹3.43 lakh crore in FY 2020-21 to ₹5.38 lakh crore in FY 2021-22, representing a growth of 56.9 per cent. The main component of this increase was in Economic Services (₹1.82 lakh crore), which included increased expenditure on Indian Railways (₹0.87 lakh crore), Civil Aviation (₹0.62 lakh crore), and Roads and Bridges (₹0.25 lakh crore).
key_facts: Capital expenditure was ₹5.38 lakh crore in 2021-22
Increased from ₹3.43 lakh crore in 2020-21
Growth rate was 56.9 per cent
Major increase in Economic Services of ₹1.82 lakh crore
Indian Railways increase was ₹0.87 lakh crore

question: What was the GDP growth rate in 2021-22?

expected.answer_summary: The GDP at current prices (Nominal GDP) in 2021-22 was ₹236.65 lakh crore with a growth rate of 19.51 per cent over the previous year. The GDP at constant (2011-12) prices (Real GDP) was ₹147.36 lakh crore with a growth rate of 8.68 per cent over the previous year.
key_facts: Nominal GDP in 2021-22 was ₹236.65 lakh crore
Nominal GDP growth rate was 19.51 per cent
Real GDP was ₹147.36 lakh crore
Real GDP growth rate was 8.68 per cent

question: What was the debt sustainability indicator in 2021-22 and what does it signify?

expected.answer_summary: The debt stabilization indicator turned positive at 8.89 in 2021-22, which is a positive indicator towards stability. A positive debt stabilization indicator means the Debt-GDP ratio would eventually fall. The indicator is calculated as Quantum Spread plus Primary Deficit. In 2021-22, the Quantum Spread was 18.23 and Primary Deficit was -9.34 (negative). The interest paid on debt was ₹8.28 lakh crore and interest payment to revenue receipt ratio improved to 33.99 per cent from 38.66 per cent in 2020-21.
key_facts: Debt stabilization indicator was 8.89 in 2021-22
Positive indicator means Debt-GDP ratio would eventually fall
Quantum Spread was 18.23
Primary Deficit was -9.34
Interest paid on debt was ₹8.28 lakh crore

key_amounts.context: Interest payment to revenue receipt ratio

question: What are the components of Extra Budgetary Resources (EBR) and what issues were identified?

expected.answer_summary: Extra Budgetary Resources are financial liabilities raised by PSUs for which repayment is done from Union Government Budget. EBRs are mobilized through Government fully serviced bonds and loans from NSSF. Key issues identified include: (1) Long Term Irrigation Fund was overstated by ₹9 crore for Polavaram Project funding; (2) LTIF loan of ₹55,438 crore was to be serviced 64.8% by NABARD and 35.2% by Government, with ₹1,445.43 crore principal serviced by Government not deducted from Central Government Debt; (3) IRFC utilized EBR of ₹71,065.90 crore in 2021-22 which was excluded from Statement 27 disclosure.
key_facts: EBR disclosed through Government fully serviced bonds and NSSF loans
LTIF overstatement of ₹9 crore for Polavaram Project
LTIF total loan was ₹55,438 crore
Government serviced ₹1,445.43 crore not deducted from Central Government Debt
IRFC EBR of ₹71,065.90 crore excluded from Statement 27

question: What were the back-to-back loans given to States in lieu of GST Compensation shortfall?

expected.answer_summary: In lieu of shortfall in GST Compensation, Government of India extended back-to-back loans of ₹1.10 lakh crore during 2020-21 and ₹1.59 lakh crore during 2021-22 to States/UTs. These loans were to be fully serviced by GoI and recouped from future Compensation Cess collections.
key_facts: Back-to-back loan in 2020-21 was ₹1.10 lakh crore
Back-to-back loan in 2021-22 was ₹1.59 lakh crore
Loans to be fully serviced by GoI
To be recouped from future Compensation Cess collections

question: What were the inconsistencies found in disclosure Form D-4 regarding financial assets?

expected.answer_summary: Significant differences were found in Form D-4 (Asset Register) regarding financial assets. For Loans to State and Union Territories, Form D-4 showed ₹0.05 lakh crore while Statement No.1(ii) of Receipt Budget 2023-24 and UGFA 2021-22 showed ₹4.92 lakh crore - a difference of ₹4.87 lakh crore. For Loans to Foreign Governments, Form D-4 showed ₹0.16 lakh crore against ₹0.15 lakh crore in UGFA, a difference of ₹0.01 lakh crore.
key_facts: Loans to States/UTs in D-4 was ₹0.05 lakh crore
UGFA showed ₹4.92 lakh crore for Loans to States/UTs
Difference was ₹4.87 lakh crore
Loans to Foreign Governments showed ₹0.01 lakh crore difference

key_amounts.context: Loans to States/UTs as per D-4
Loans to States/UTs as per UGFA

question: What guarantee-related omissions were found in disclosure statements?

expected.answer_summary: Two guarantee-related omissions were found: (1) A single default guarantee of ₹9,495 crore issued to Department of Food & Public Distribution (after merger of pulses guarantee of ₹500 crore) valid up to 31.03.2022 was not disclosed in footnote in D-3; (2) An amount of ₹1,770.84 crore relating to nine guarantees that was invoked during the year and shown as 'Discharged' by Department of Financial Services was not properly disclosed. The Ministry advised the concerned departments to disclose such details in Form D3 footnotes.
key_facts: Single default guarantee of ₹9,495 crore not disclosed
Pulses guarantee of ₹500 crore was merged in food guarantee
₹1,770.84 crore invoked relating to nine guarantees
Ministry advised disclosure in D3 footnotes

question: What was the trend in expenditure on Health and Family Welfare sector in 2021-22?

expected.answer_summary: Expenditure on Health and Family Welfare increased to ₹70,236 crore in 2021-22, representing a 107.3 per cent increase over 2020-21 when it was ₹33,884 crore. This was part of the Government's focus on health as a priority sector as stated in MTFP 2021-22.
key_facts: Health and Family Welfare expenditure was ₹70,236 crore in 2021-22
Increase of 107.3 per cent over previous year
2020-21 expenditure was ₹33,884 crore
Health was identified as a focus sector

question: What was the variation found in the annuity project disclosure for New Police Headquarters?

expected.answer_summary: For the project 'Development of new police headquarters at Jai Singh Road, Parliament Street, New Delhi' under Ministry of Home Affairs, a variation of ₹164.8 crore was found in Form D-5 disclosure. The total annuity committed was stated as ₹1,178.80 crore, but based on annual annuity of ₹78 crore for 13 years, the calculated value should be ₹1,014 crore. The project value was ₹286 crore. The Ministry explained that variation was due to pre-payment of two installments (₹80.80 crore) and lump sum payment of ₹84 crore towards change of scope.
key_facts: Project value was ₹286 crore
Total Annuity Committed stated as ₹1,178.80 crore
Annual Annuity was ₹78 crore for 13 years
Calculated value should be ₹1,014 crore
Variation was ₹164.8 crore
Variation due to pre-payment and lump sum payment

question: How many times has the FRBM Act been amended since its enactment and what are the key fiscal targets?

expected.answer_summary: The FRBM Act was enacted on 26 August 2003 and has been amended four times - in 2004, 2012, 2015, and 2018. The key targets set by the 2018 Amendment Act (to be achieved by end of FY 2024-25) are: General Government debt not to exceed 60 per cent of GDP, Central Government debt not to exceed 40 per cent of GDP, and additional guarantees not to exceed one-half per cent (0.5%) of GDP in any financial year. The fiscal deficit target was to reduce to 3 per cent of GDP by 31 March 2021, and Government has committed to reach below 4.5 per cent by FY 2025-26.
key_facts: FRBM Act enacted on 26 August 2003
Amended four times: 2004, 2012, 2015, 2018
General Government debt target: 60 per cent of GDP by 2024-25
Central Government debt target: 40 per cent of GDP by 2024-25
Additional guarantees limit: 0.5 per cent of GDP per year
Fiscal deficit target below 4.5 per cent by FY 2025-26

question: What was the outcome of the mid-year review for 2021-22 under FRBM Act?

expected.answer_summary: The mid-year review for 2021-22 showed compliance with all FRBM benchmarks. Non-Debt Receipt at mid-year was ₹10.99 lakh crore (55.60% of BE of ₹19.76 lakh crore), exceeding the minimum benchmark of 40%. Revenue Deficit was ₹3.16 lakh crore (27.70% of BE of ₹11.41 lakh crore), well below the maximum 70% benchmark. Fiscal Deficit was ₹5.27 lakh crore (35.00% of BE of ₹15.07 lakh crore), also below the maximum 70% benchmark. There were no adverse variations.
key_facts: Non-Debt Receipt at mid-year was 55.60% of BE (benchmark: not less than 40%)
Revenue Deficit was 27.70% of BE (benchmark: not more than 70%)
Fiscal Deficit was 35.00% of BE (benchmark: not more than 70%)
All benchmarks were met with no adverse variations

question: What percentage of total expenditure was financed by non-debt receipts in 2021-22?

expected.answer_summary: In 2021-22, total expenditure financed by Non-Debt Receipts was 58.41 per cent. The Non-Debt Receipts were ₹24.76 lakh crore against total expenditure of ₹42.39 lakh crore. The year-on-year increase in non-debt receipts was 28.16 per cent, while total expenditure increased by 8.47 per cent. This was an improvement from 2020-21 when only 49.44 per cent of expenditure was financed by non-debt receipts.
key_facts: 58.41 per cent of total expenditure financed by non-debt receipts
Non-Debt Receipts were ₹24.76 lakh crore
Total Expenditure was ₹42.39 lakh crore
Improvement from 49.44 per cent in 2020-21

key_amounts.context: Total expenditure financed by Non-Debt Receipts
